notes: Common informal greeting, borrowed from English 'howdy'. Confirmed across multiple independent phrasebook sources (Wikivoyage's Sranan phrasebook, Wikitravel's Sranan phrasebook, kaikki.org's Sranan Tongo dictionary) as 'greetings / good day', used on its own or extended as 'Odi, mastra/misi'.
Literally 'how('s your) walk?' — a very common casual way of asking how someone is doing. Confirmed in Wikivoyage's and Wikitravel's Sranan phrasebooks.

Literally 'how do you stay/be?' — a more neutral, slightly less colloquial way of asking how someone is doing than 'Fa waka?', the two being roughly interchangeable register variants. Confirmed in Wikivoyage's Sranan phrasebook and the Peace Corps Sranan Tongo pre-departure training materials.

translations.nl: het gaat goed / ik ben goed

notes: Standard reply to 'Fa waka?' / 'Fa yu tan?'. Confirmed in Wikitravel's and Wikivoyage's Sranan phrasebooks; a fuller variant 'Mi de bun' ('I am well') is also attested with the same meaning.

Everyday, informal 'thanks'. Confirmed via the Glosbe Sranan Tongo–English dictionary entry ('tangi' = gratitude / thank / thanks) and the Wikitravel/Wikivoyage phrasebooks, which list the fuller 'Gran tangi' as the more formal counterpart.

Literally 'big thanks' — the more formal/emphatic counterpart to plain 'Tangi', used the way Sarnami distinguishes the casual 'Rām Rām' from the more formal 'Pranām'. Confirmed in both the Wikitravel and Wikivoyage Sranan phrasebooks.
